name: domestic-violence-safety
description: Safety planning, shelter access, protective order process, financial separation from abusive partners, and confidential communication. The full safety ecosystem beyond just the legal instrument.
source: Liberation Labs mutual aid knowledge base

triples.subject: National Domestic Violence Hotline
triples.object: 1-800-799-7233 (SAFE) or text START to 88788 — 24/7 confidential support, safety planning, and local referrals

triples.subject: safety plan
triples.object: identifying safe rooms, packed emergency bag, copies of important documents, memorized phone numbers, code word with trusted person, and planned escape route

triples.subject: emergency protective order
triples.object: without the abuser being notified initially — issued by a judge, typically valid 5-7 days until a full hearing

triples.subject: address confidentiality program
triples.object: a substitute mailing address so the survivor's actual location is not in public records — available in most states through the Secretary of State

triples.subject: safe phone plan
triples.object: a separate account NOT on the abuser's family plan — prepaid phones, Lifeline program phones, or shelter-provided phones

triples.subject: digital safety
triples.object: checking for tracking apps/spyware, using private browsing, creating new email accounts, and being aware that smart home devices can be used for surveillance

triples.subject: Victims of Crime Act (VOCA) fund
triples.object: relocation costs, lost wages, medical expenses, counseling, and legal fees for crime victims — apply through state victim compensation program

triples.subject: shelter
triples.object: safe housing, meals, case management, legal advocacy, children's services, and support groups — locations are confidential

triples.subject: safety plan for leaving
triples.object: ID, birth certificates, Social Security cards, insurance info, medication, children's school records, evidence of abuse (photos, police reports), cash, and a change of clothes

triples.subject: financial separation
triples.object: opening a separate bank account, securing credit in own name, documenting all shared assets, changing direct deposit, and freezing joint credit cards

triples.subject: workplace safety
triples.object: informing HR/security (confidentially), requesting schedule changes, varying routes to work, and state laws protecting DV survivors from employment discrimination

triples.subject: children in DV situations
triples.object: custody orders should be obtained quickly after separation, school safety plans established, and children may need age-appropriate counseling

triples.subject: immigrant survivors
triples.object: U visa (for crime victims), VAWA self-petition (no need for abuser's cooperation), and T visa for trafficking victims — immigration status should NOT prevent seeking help

triples.subject: pet safety
triples.object: SAF-T program (Sheltering Animals and Families Together), local animal shelters with DV pet housing, or trusted friend/family member — abusers often threaten pets

triples.subject: evidence documentation
triples.object: photograph injuries with timestamps, save threatening messages/voicemails, keep a written log with dates/details, and store copies outside the home (email to trusted person or cloud)

triples.subject: strangulation
triples.object: survivors who have been strangled are 750% more likely to be killed — seek immediate medical evaluation even without visible injury, as internal damage may be present

triples.subject: protection order violation
triples.object: police immediately — violations are criminal offenses, document every violation even if police response is slow

triples.subject: DV and substance use
triples.object: substance use programs should screen for DV; DV programs should not require sobriety — integrated services exist, and either issue can be addressed first

triples.subject: lethality assessment
triples.object: prior strangulation, threats to kill, access to weapons, escalating violence, stalking, forced sex, controlling behavior, and separation/divorce (leaving is the highest-risk period)

triples.subject: coordinator
triples.object: believe the survivor, don't pressure them to leave before they're ready, safety plan for staying AND leaving, never contact the abuser on the survivor's behalf, and respect their autonomy
